danger_signals.visual_form: Character animation becomes slower, stumbling motions; visual damage indicators on character sprite
danger_signals.meaning: Opponent or player is weakened and vulnerable to finishing moves

danger_signals.visual_form: Referee count-up display when wrestler is outside ring (reaches 20-count threshold)
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent loss condition if wrestler remains outside ring

danger_signals.visual_form: In two-player mode: momentum meter rapidly swings toward one player
danger_signals.meaning: Current grappling advantage held by opponent; danger of being thrown or pinned

danger_signals.visual_form: Blue King character introduction after defeating all playable wrestlers
danger_signals.meaning: Ultimate challenge and danger threshold in campaign progression

safety_signals.visual_form: Seven spheres above training meter that fill up during button-mashing exercises
safety_signals.meaning: Increased move power and damage output; player is preparing and strengthening

safety_signals.visual_form: Three-count pinfall completion, submission acceptance, or opponent count-out
safety_signals.meaning: Wrestler has successfully defeated opponent and advanced

safety_signals.visual_form: Momentum meter swings toward player's side in two-player mode
safety_signals.meaning: Player has grappling advantage and favorable positioning

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Wrestler birthplace and nationality (Japan, Mexico, Greece, UK, Hong Kong, Soviet Union, West Germany, Ecuador, Venezuela, USA)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each wrestler has distinct sprite and fighting style reflecting nationality
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Wrestler selection screen displays birthplace; commentary references national origin

faction_indicators.faction: Blue King (Earl of Doom) - final antagonist
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Unique non-playable character with distinctive appearance
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Blue King uses every wrestler's technique set; visually set apart from playable roster
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Appears only after defeating all other wrestlers; positioned as ultimate opposition

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Wrestler roster cycling through opponents; visual presentation of each international fighter
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Each opponent wrestler uses different move sets and specialties; difficulty or strategy adjusts per opponent

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Seven fillable spheres above training meter; trainer character performing exercises (squats, situps, push-ups with sumo wrestler weight)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Move damage output increases with sphere completion; loss of match results in sphere loss requiring re-training

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Two-player mode momentum meter that visually swings between players
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Grappling advantage dynamically shifts; player with momentum advantage gains control in holds and throws

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Japanese version: announcer 'Yasushi Geki' with black hair and Japanese features; US/EU version: 'Tom Talker' with different appearance and English commentary
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Japanese version uses password system for progress tracking; regional translation differences in move names and announcer speech

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character animation becomes visually slower and stumbling; sprite shows damage accumulation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Weakened wrestler becomes vulnerable to signature finishing moves; action replay cutscenes trigger for signature moves on weakened opponents

environmental_storytelling.example: Akira Dragon (Japan/Samurai) uses Back Brain Kick and German Suplex; El Tigre (Mexico/Striker) uses Back Drop and Northern Right Suplex; Boris Chekov (Soviet Union/Siberian Machine) uses Giant Swing and Power Slam
Wrestlers are identified by international origin and fighting archetype (e.g., Jackie Lee as Kung Fu Master from Hong Kong, Pat Gordon as Greek Warrior, Dr. Guildo as The Technician from USA)
Between-match training sequences show player character performing physical conditioning with visual trainer character; sphere progression system indicates preparation and readiness
When signature finishing moves are performed on weakened wrestlers, game cuts to close-up replay view simulating television broadcast presentation, heightening moment significance
Animated Tom Talker (US) or Yasushi Geki (JP) provides play-by-play commentary through text bubbles, creating broadcast atmosphere and communicating match events

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Full speed animations; maximum move damage output when training spheres are full
Slow stumbling animations; loss of training sphere upon defeat; vulnerable to signature move action replays
Normal grappling speed and throw execution; standard damage output
Visible damage accumulation in sprite; slower animation speed; susceptible to finishing moves triggering action replay cutscenes
Uses complete move set from all playable wrestlers; positions as ultimate multi-dimensional threat
Count-out timer initiates; visual count display; disqualification if count reaches 20 before return to ring
Momentum meter swing indicates which player controls grapple outcome; advantage determines throw and hold success

notes: Tecmo World Wrestling is a professional wrestling simulation that communicates world state primarily through wrestler nationality/faction identity, move specialization, visual condition states (health/weakening), and training progression. The game uses international wrestler archetypes as its primary factional system rather than explicit rival organizations. Danger is signaled through health depletion animations and count-out mechanics. World state shifts are communicated through training sphere completion, opponent cycling in campaign mode, and momentum meter visualization in multiplayer. The action replay system for signature moves on weakened opponents creates a dynamic visual response to match state. Regional differences (Japanese vs. US/EU announcer and password system) suggest localization of presentation rather than fundamental world-state changes.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Tecmo World Wrestling article)
